title: SubTrack++ : Gradient Subspace Tracking for Scalable LLM Training
abstract: Training large language models (LLMs) is highly resource-intensive due to their massive number of parameters and the overhead of optimizer states. While recent work has aimed to reduce memory consumption, such efforts often entail trade-offs among memory efficiency, training time, and model performance. Yet, true democratization of LLMs requires simultaneous progress across all three dimensions. To this end, we propose SubTrack++ that leverages Grassmannian gradient subspace tracking combined with projection-aware optimizers, enabling Adam’s internal statistics to adapt to subspace changes. Additionally, employing recovery scaling, a technique that restores information lost through low-rank projections, further enhances model performance. Our method demonstrates SOTA convergence by exploiting Grassmannian geometry, **reducing training wall-time by up to 65%** compared to the best performing baseline, LDAdam, while preserving the reduced memory footprint.  Code is at https://github.com/criticalml-uw/SubTrack.

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper proposes SubTrack++, a memory- and time-efficient method for scalable low-rank optimization in large language model (LLM) training. SubTrack++ addresses the memory bottleneck in LLMs by combining three key components: (1) Grassmannian subspace tracking for adaptively estimating low-rank projection spaces without relying on computationally expensive SVD, (2) a projection-aware optimizer that incorporates subspace changes into the momentum update rules of Adam, and (3) a recovery scaling mechanism to recover information lost during low-rank projections. The paper provides theoretical convergence guarantees and validates the method through extensive experiments across LLaMA-based pretraining, GLUE/SuperGLUE fine-tuning, and synthetic robustness benchmarks. Results show that SubTrack++ outperforms existing low-rank methods like GaLore and FIRA in terms of loss, memory, and wall-time, while approaching full-rank performance. The method is also demonstrated to be robust to abrupt subspace shifts and scale changes.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
Strengths:
The paper presents a well-motivated, efficient, and robust method for scalable LLM training, combining geometry-aware subspace tracking, optimizer adaptation, and recovery scaling, and validates its effectiveness through rigorous theoretical and empirical analysis.

Weaknesses:

1. Limited architectural and task diversity in experiments
Most experiments are conducted on encoder-only models (e.g., LLaMA-1/2) and primarily within language modeling and GLUE-style tasks. The method has not been tested on decoder-only architectures, vision-language models, or instruction tuning scenarios. This limits the conclusions one can draw about its general applicability to a wider range of model types and tasks.
2. Unverified scalability beyond 7B models
Although SubTrack++ is shown to be effective up to 7B parameter models, the paper does not investigate its behavior on larger-scale models such as 13B or 65B. Without insights into numerical stability, memory behavior, and performance at such scales, the practicality of the method for the largest LLMs remains uncertain.

QUESTIONS:
1. Have you considered applying SubTrack++ to decoder-only or multimodal architectures?
The current experimental scope is focused on encoder-only LLaMA models and text classification tasks (e.g., GLUE/SuperGLUE). Given the rising importance of decoder-only and multimodal models (e.g., LLaVA, Flamingo), it would be useful to understand whether SubTrack++ can be seamlessly applied to these architectures. Any clarification or additional discussion on its architectural flexibility would strengthen the applicability claim.
2. Why not include a quantitative analysis of projection error or subspace drift over time?
The paper emphasizes the robustness of SubTrack++ in tracking subspace changes, yet it lacks quantitative measures such as geodesic distance or angle between subspaces to support this claim. Could the authors provide an empirical analysis of subspace drift over the training trajectory? This would help substantiate the claimed robustness benefits and differentiate SubTrack++ from SVD-based methods more clearly.

LIMITATIONS:
While the paper presents strong empirical and theoretical results, there are several limitations that merit discussion:

Limited Architectural Coverage:
All experiments are conducted on encoder-only architectures, including LLaMA and RoBERTa. The method's performance on decoder-only models or multimodal systems—common in generative modeling and vision-language tasks—remains untested, raising questions about its generality across architecture types.

Scalability Beyond 7B Models:
SubTrack++ is evaluated on models up to 7 billion parameters. However, its behavior at larger scales—such as memory overhead, subspace tracking stability, and optimization dynamics in 13B or 65B models—has not been explored. These aspects are crucial to assess its viability for full-scale deployment in industrial LLM settings.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper proposes SubTrack++, a novel memory-efficient algorithm for LLM training using gradient projection. SubTrack++ is composed of several key components: i) the novel subspace tracking mechanism based on the Grassmannian manifold; ii) the momentum inheritance mechanism similar to LDAdam; iii) the recovery scaling mechanism similar to Fira. Combining the above techniques, SubTrack++ can not only surpass existing algorithms in training performance, but also be more memory and computation-efficient.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
Strengths:

i) The proposed algorithm exhibits great performance in the experiment, both in training performance and in training efficiency.

ii) The overall paper is well presented and easy to follow.

iii) The effectiveness of the proposed subspace tracking mechanism over GaLore's method is well addressed by illustrative examples.

Weakness:

i) While the experimental performance is impressive, this paper somehow lacks more incentive analysis on why it can beat existing algorithms. The proposed analysis is based on a strong assumption that the gradient matrix is as simple as a linear transform of the weight matrix, and the result only accounts for convergence on the projected gradient, which may not be so informative as a convergence result on the original gradient.

ii) The model used in the fine-tuning experiment is pretty small. I acknowledge this may follow the same setting as GaLore's paper; however, in my experience, existing algorithms (using gradient projection) behave poorly in fine-tuning large models such as LLaMA-7B. Given the promising performance of SubTrack++, it should be a good choice to validate its advantage in this scenario.

QUESTIONS:
In the provided source code, I did not find codes that support the error feedback mechanism in LDAdam (Maybe I've just missed it). It seems that the code for reproducing the results regarding LDAdam (or other baselines not supported by the provided codes) is not provided, so I wonder if they are really not provided. If so, have you reproduced their algorithms or used the publicly reported data?

LIMITATIONS:
The authors have addressed the limitations, including the constraints on using the Adam family and the scale of their experiment.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
The authors tackle the problem of online subspace tracking by formulating it as minimizing a reconstruction cost on the Grassmann manifold. They introduce a lightweight rank-1 geodesic update that approximates the full tangent by its leading singular component, then moves the current subspace along the corresponding principal plane via a closed-form rotation. This approach avoids heavy matrix exponentials or full SVD at each step, yielding an algorithm that is both computationally efficient and easy to implement. Empirical results on synthetic and real-world streaming datasets demonstrate that the proposed method matches or outperforms state-of-the-art trackers in accuracy, while reducing per-update cost.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
Strengths:

1. Clear derivation of the rank-1 geodesic step with full orthonormality proof on Grassmann. Solid empirical evaluation across multiple benchmarks; ablation shows robustness to step-size and noise.

3. Well-written exposition of manifold basics and geodesic construction. Pseudocode aligns closely with the released PyTorch code.

Weakness:

1. Some details of the update rule is not well discussed (see Questions below).

2. No ablation study on the subspace update frequency and the rank chosen for subspace update.

QUESTIONS:
1. For the subspace tracking step, I think you want to use the gradient method to minimize the cost function (2) on the Grassmann Manifold. However, the algorithm performs an SVD on $\nabla F$ rather than on $-\nabla F$? It seems like the algorithm is doing a gradient ascent on the Grassmann Manifold. Could the authors clarify why the SVD is taken on $\nabla F$ instead of $- \nabla F$?

2. Why there exists a term $1 - \beta_2^{t-1}$ in (9)? Suppose that $S_t = S_{t-1}$, the update rule for the first-moment Projection-Aware Optimizer step is reduced to regular adam like update, but it is not the same case for the second-moment. What caused such inconsistence?

3. Since the proposed method is much more efficient that full SVD of the gradient, why the authors don't try a more frequent update to the subspace or a higher rank update?

LIMITATIONS:
yes.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
The authors study memory efficient large language model training (LLM) through low rank projections. They propose Sub Track++, which combines Grassmannian gradient subspace tracking, Projection-aware optimization, and recovery scaling. They provide theoretical guarantee on the convergence of the subspace and the empirical evaluation shows the improved performance over the compared baselines.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
The strength of the paper is that the proposed method by itself is decent. All the proposed mechanisms are reasonable and accompanied by theoretical guarantees and empirical improvements.

The main weakness is the presentation and the discussion/comparison with the related works. While the proposed method itself is reasonable, it is hard for readers to understand why it should work better than the existing ones. I summarized my issues as follows.

1. Figure 1 looks like a very unconventional way to present the statistics the authors desire to present.

2. There seems to be no in-depth discussion even with the baseline methods mentioned in the paper. This is concerning especially when combined with the ablation results. It seems like Grassmannian gradient subspace tracking by itself does not perform better than Galore, while Projection-aware optimization and recovery scaling are inspired by LDAdam and Fira respectively.

3. There are also some related works in the literature that can be discussed. For example LoRo [1] also utilizes Riemannian optimization for subspace tracking, while LoRA-RITE [2] also utilizes Projection-aware optimization on the momentum with a seemingly different formulation.

4. Some empirical results look counterintuitive. Specifically, the larger models perform worse than the smaller one, which might indicate that suboptimal settings are being used. Additionally, SubTrack++ sometimes outperforms the full-rank training, which also requires some explanation. 



[1] Mo, Z., Huang, L. K., & Pan, S. J. (2025). Parameter and memory efficient pretraining via low-rank riemannian optimization. In The Thirteenth International Conference on Learning Representations.

[2] Yen, J. N., Si, S., Meng, Z., Yu, F., Duvvuri, S. S., Dhillon, I. S., ... & Kumar, S. (2024). LoRA Done RITE: Robust Invariant Transformation Equilibration for LoRA Optimization. The Thirteenth International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR 2025)

QUESTIONS:
My questions are related to the weakness listed. Please provide some insights on why the proposed method should perform better than the compared baselines. Additionally, please explain why in the experiments larger models seem to perform worse than the smaller ones and why subtrack++ can outperform full rank training.

LIMITATIONS:
The authors have some discussion in the conclusion section.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
DECISION:
Accept (poster)

COMMENT:
This was a rare case where the author response made some reviewers better appreciate the contribution and some reviewers appreciate the contribution less. So my uncertainty is high here, but all reviewers ultimately gave a recommending score so that seems like the most logical recommendation.
reviews.TITLE: Decision
